does: This paper systematically audits the reliability of SAE quality metrics used in SAEBench, the standard benchmarking suite for sparse autoencoders. The authors evaluate metrics through three complementary lenses: reseed noise (variability on fixed SAEs), ground-truth correlation on synthetic SAEs with known properties, and discriminability across SAE training trajectories. They find that Targeted Probe Perturbation and Spurious Correlation Removal metrics fail multiple reliability tests and should not be used for SAE evaluation. The remaining metrics exhibit higher reseed noise and lower discriminability than previously assumed, with sae-probes emerging as the most reliable but still unable to reliably distinguish between variants of the same architecture. The paper demonstrates fundamental gaps in current SAE evaluation methodology.
connects: This work extends the growing field of mechanistic interpretability and sparse autoencoders, which have emerged as key tools for understanding neural network internals following recent advances in SAE architecture and training. The paper builds on SAEBench itself and prior work establishing SAEs as interpretability methods, but shifts focus from developing new SAE variants to critically examining the benchmarks used to evaluate them. It engages with broader concerns in machine learning about benchmark reliability and metric validity, following patterns of benchmark auditing that have challenged other evaluation suites. The work responds to implicit assumptions in the field that existing metrics reliably rank SAE quality, connecting to foundational questions about measurement in interpretability research.
enables: This audit establishes that the field urgently needs improved SAE benchmarks and evaluation methodology. The findings motivate development of more robust metrics that minimize reseed noise, better correlate with ground truth properties, and demonstrate higher discriminability across SAE variants. Future work could explore alternative evaluation approaches such as task-based metrics, adversarial evaluation, or human-aligned assessment of interpretability quality. The identification of which metrics partially succeed points toward improving those approaches rather than wholesale replacement. More broadly, this work enables more reliable progress on SAE architectures by preventing the field from optimizing toward unreliable proxies, and sets a template for auditing interpretability evaluation methods more generally as the field scales.
